name: MoarVM GC — Generational, Parallel STW, Per-Thread Nursery
summary: Raku's MoarVM ships a generational, parallel, precise, moving GC. 'Parallel' means stop-the-world but multi-thread cooperation on the collection — not concurrent or mostly-concurrent in the ZGC / Shenandoah / Go sense.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Raku's **MoarVM** ships a generational, parallel, precise, *moving* GC. "Parallel" here means stop-the-world but multi-thread cooperation on the collection — *not* concurrent or mostly-concurrent in the ZGC / Shenandoah / Go sense.

The nursery is per-thread (semi-space copying for cheap death-young behaviour); the old generation uses sized buckets with large-object special-casing.

sections.title: Cross-thread reachability via in-tray
sections.content:
Cross-thread reachability is handled by passing `ThreadWork` / `WorkToPass` structures via a per-thread "**in-tray**" (`add_in_tray_to_worklist`) — the GC's analogue of work-passing across mutator threads, distinct from a traditional remembered-set + write-barrier approach.

The in-tray is a queue of work items each thread holds for incoming cross-thread reachability information. When thread A creates a reference into thread B's nursery, the corresponding work item is dropped into B's in-tray; B processes its in-tray as part of its next GC cycle. The design avoids a global write barrier table at the cost of more cross-thread bookkeeping during collection.

sections.title: PR #1861 — dedicated nursery memory area
sections.content:
The in-flight PR #1861 ("Dedicated nursery memory area") reserves a single ~1 GB virtual region at startup for nursery allocation so the **nursery-membership test on the write barrier collapses to a bounds check** — eliminating pointer-following on the hot path.

Even without colored pointers or load-reference barriers, an aggressive per-thread nursery plus bounds-check write barrier delivers respectable latency on a workload (Raku) where most allocations die young.
